[JS Weird World] No.44 Functional Programming (Part 2)
JsWeirdWorld
JavaScript

Once you've got the functional programming concepts down, where do you go next? This post points you at two classic JavaScript utility libraries — underscore.js and Lodash. The idea is to read the open-source code and work backwards from it, until the concepts turn into muscle memory.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/21/jsweirdworld-43-functional-programming-01/
[JS Weird World] No.43 Functional Programming (Part 1)

Why is JavaScript closer to Lisp and Scheme than to Java? In this post we use first-class functions to build a little mapForEach, and walk through how functional programming helps you wrap up repetitive work into something clean and reusable so you write less and do more.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/21/jsweirdworld-42-call-apply-bind/
[JS Weird World] No.42 call(), apply() and bind() — Controlling this Yourself

Time to dig into three methods every JavaScript function carries around: call(), apply() and bind(). They all tie back to this and the arguments you pass in. We'll see how bind copies a function and locks this, how call runs it on the spot, how apply does the same but takes an array, and finish with real use cases like function borrowing and function currying.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/20/js-weird-world-40-function-factories/
[JS Weird World] No.40 A Framework Heads-Up: Function Factories

Closures are useful in a lot of places in JavaScript. In this post we'll look at how to use closures to build something a little special — even patterns that look impossible at first glance. The trick is called function factories.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/20/js-weird-world-39-closures-loop-variable/
[JS Weird World] No.39 Closures, Part 2: Why Your Loop Always Logs the Same Number

When you search for closure examples online, you'll keep running into the same loop-and-function snippet — and if the concept hasn't clicked yet, it suddenly feels really hard. In this post I walk through the classic "why does my loop always log 3?" puzzle, then three different ways to actually fix it (let, IIFE, and the textbook IIFE-with-argument trick).
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/20/js-weird-world-38-closures-part-1/
[JS Weird World] No.38 Closures (Part 1)

This chapter kicks off one of the scariest, most notorious topics in JavaScript — closures. If we want to really understand this language, closures are a must-know concept. A lot of people get confused by closures, or honestly just hate them, because they're genuinely hard to wrap your head around. In this post we walk through what's actually happening under the hood.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/20/jsweirdworld-37-iife-safe-code/
[JS Weird World] No.37 A Framework Tip: IIFEs and Safe Code

We've already covered IIFEs earlier in this series, and a lot of well-known frameworks lean on this technique. So in this post I want to dig into IIFEs, safe code, and why this pattern is so useful — like keeping your variables from leaking into the global scope and avoiding nasty collisions.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/20/js-weird-world-36-iife/
[JS Weird World] No.36 Immediately Invoked Function Expressions (IIFEs)

An Immediately Invoked Function Expression, or IIFE, is something a lot of people have already run into. But what actually is an IIFE? How does it work, and what's it good for? That's exactly what this chapter digs into — including why wrapping a function in parens makes the syntax parser happy.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/19/jsweirdworld-35-whitespace/
[JS Weird World] No.35 A Framework Reminder: Whitespace

Whitespace — the invisible characters that create space. Things like Enter, Tab, and Space help make our code more readable, and they don't get executed by the engine. JavaScript is super relaxed about whitespace, which means we can use it to do some handy things, like dropping comments in unusual spots.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/19/jsweirdworld-33-syntax-parser/
[JS Weird World] No.33 Quick Reminder: The Syntax Parser

We touched on this topic way back, but it's worth bringing up again to reinforce the idea — because the stuff coming up next is all tied to the syntax parser. This post is a quick reminder of what the syntax parser actually does and why it matters.
